text: The surgeon uses a right-sided approach, inserting the superior port after placing the camera. The FlexDex needle holder is introduced for the operation.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of the FlexDex needle holder, showing how it allows full wristed motion. The needle holder is inserted directly through the soft tissue in the chest without using a port.
The surgeon inserts a third port, accommodating a five-millimeter instrument, into the chest cavity during the VATS thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses the ligature device to secure tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the ligature device, visible through the third port, to manipulate and divide tissue during the VATS thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses the hook to pull tissue towards themselves, demonstrating the utility of the hook in manipulating anatomical structures during the VATS thymectomy.
The surgeon uses a FlexDex instrument to grasp and clear the thymus from the diaphragmatic side, beginning the dissection by separating the thymus from the diaphragm and moving upwards.
The surgeon has cleared the thymus from the diaphragmatic surface and begins to separate it from the lateral portion of the pericardium using a grasper.
The surgeon uses a precise grasp to lift and divide the thymus tissue from the lateral portion of the pericardium.
The surgeon strips the thymus from the surrounding tissue, encountering a small vessel at the inferior pole, which may be a pericardial branch.
The surgeon identifies multiple blood supplies to the thymus, including inferior, lateral, and superior sources, which are visually removed during the procedure.

The surgeon opens the pleural reflection over the pericardium, revealing the underlying structures. The video includes a cutaway view demonstrating the comfortable positioning of the surgical instruments.
The surgeon positions their arm at a right angle to achieve an optimal grasp on the thymus tissue, using the Ligasure device to perform precise divisions of the tissue.
The surgeon courses upwards towards the superior vena cava, using the FlexDex instrument to lift the surrounding tissue while approaching the phrenic nerve.
The surgeon exposes the phrenic nerve, visible as a distinct structure within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the ligasure device to carefully dissect near the phrenic nerve, minimizing the risk of damage due to the precise heating confined between the jaws, with radial heating extending less than a millimeter.

The surgeon divides tissue just in front of the phrenic nerve, extending the dissection upwards along the superior vena cava past the reamer vein and the right internal mammary vein. The pulsating reamer vein is visible above the flex decks needle holder, while the pleura is opened just beyond this point.
The surgeon dissects the rhema vein, marking the far lateral extent of the thymus excision during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon switches from using a ligature to a hook instrument, following a path just medial to the pulsating rhema vein.

The surgeon dissects the thymus off the posterior surface of the sternum, aiming to remove as much fatty tissue as possible. The dissection proceeds medially, using the reamer vein as the lateral boundary, and continues across the pericardium just above the diaphragm.
The surgeon opens the pleura on the opposite side, revealing the underlying structures and facilitating further dissection during the thymectomy procedure.

The surgeon opens the pleura to access the lateral thoracic cavity, preparing to insert a sub-xiphoid port for the removal of the thymus. A 30-degree camera is positioned through the sub-xiphoid port, providing a clear view of the left phrenic nerve.
The surgeon dissects the reamer vein, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a FlexDex instrument to pull the superior horn of the thymus medially, effectively extracting it from the neck with the aid of a high-quality camera view.
The innominate vein becomes visible as the surgeon uses the FlexDex needle holder to grasp the right superior horn of the thymus.
The surgeon dissects the thymus along the medial side of the superior vena cava (SVC), carefully releasing it from the posterior surface of the manubrium.

The surgeon presses down on the sternal notch to visualize the superior part of the dissection. The right superior horn is exposed, with the innominate artery visible below it. The thyro-thymic ligament and its vessels are also clearly seen in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the top of the right superior horn using the articulated FlexDex needle holder, completing the dissection of this anatomical structure.
The surgeon carefully pulls the thymus gland out of the neck area, using traction to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the dissection, moving medially to further separate the thymus from surrounding structures.
The surgical field provides a clear view of the innominate artery, positioned below the right superior horn, with the thyro-thymic ligament and its vessels visible nearby.
The surgeon searches for the trachea, adjusting the view to locate this anatomical structure within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection over the previously exposed area, carefully maneuvering around anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues to cut tissue, advancing the dissection further medially in the surgical field.

The surgeon dissects the left pleura up to the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) and LIMA vein, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissue. The LIMA vein is seen extending into the innominate vein.

The surgeon has nearly dissected the thymus into the left chest, providing natural retraction for further removal from the neck. The lima vein is visible, with the innominate vein seen beyond the ligature. The surgeon uses the flex test to pull down the structures, facilitating the dissection of the lima vein.
The surgeon identifies the phrenic nerve located just below the insertion of the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) into the innominate vein, ensuring safety by avoiding dissection below this level.
The surgeon maneuvers the thymus into the left chest cavity, utilizing the surrounding anatomical structures for retraction and positioning.
The surgeon prepares to divide additional connective tissue between the thymus and the pericardium, ensuring complete separation of the thymus from surrounding structures.
The surgeon releases the remaining attachments of the thymus to allow it to be repositioned into the left chest cavity.
The thymus is positioned into the left chest, providing natural retraction, with the innominate vein visible beyond the ligature and the LIMA vein being dissected.
The surgeon uses the FlexDex instrument to articulate and maneuver, providing a clear view for the dissection of the thymus from the pericardium.
The surgeon carefully dissects the thymus from the pericardium over the aorta, using precise movements to separate the thymus tissue from the underlying structures.
The innominate vein is visible in the surgical field during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon approaches the neck region, preparing to continue the dissection in the area surrounding the thymus.
The innominate artery is visible in the surgical field, positioned near the thymus and adjacent to the previously exposed innominate vein.
The surgeon pulls the superior horn of the thymus downward, continuing the dissection to remove it from the neck area.
The surgeon ensures the dissection remains above the thymus gland, maintaining the correct anatomical plane during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the dissection plane adjacent to the innominate artery, maintaining careful separation of tissue layers during the VATS thymectomy.
The surgeon approaches the left superior horn of the thymus, preparing to pull it down and remove it from the neck area.
The surgeon navigates over the trachea during the thymectomy procedure, maintaining the dissection plane above the thymus.
The surgeon carefully clears fatty tissue from the trachea, ensuring the trachea is fully visible and free of any surrounding adipose tissue.
The surgeon continues to dissect the left superior horn of the thymus, ensuring complete separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses flex sticks in the left hand and a hook ligature in the right hand, maneuvering around the surgical field.
The surgeon cuts across the top of the left superior horn of the thymus and proceeds to dissect it away from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon improves the visibility of the trachea, revealing its anatomical position and surrounding structures.
The surgeon begins to pull, revealing the innominate vein beyond the left superior horn, assessing the remaining tissue to be removed.
The surgeon observes the adipose tissue dipping down to the left side of the trachea, highlighting the anatomical positioning of the fat relative to the trachea.
The surgeon approaches the final stage of the procedure, where the left carotid artery becomes visible in the distance, beyond the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully extracts the left superior horn, with the innominate vein visible beyond the structure.
The left carotid artery is visible in the surgical field, positioned near the left side of the trachea.
The surgeon prepares to remove a small remaining portion of thymus tissue, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon has successfully removed the two superior horns from the neck region, completing this stage of the thymectomy procedure.
The surgical field displays the innominate artery, trachea, and left carotid artery, with an absence of fatty tissue visible in the neck region.
The surgeon releases the thymus from the anominate artery, carefully separating the tissue connections between these structures.
The surgeon identifies a small amount of tissue located between the anomalies and the pulmonary artery, slightly to the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully adheres to the pericardium while avoiding the fatty tissue of the thymus to ensure complete removal during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses Flextech instruments to grasp tissue while carefully positioning them to avoid interference with the ligature, providing clear views of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a specialized grasper to manipulate the thymus, which appears as fatty tissue, effectively aiding in the removal process.
The surgeon dissects a small thymic vein, which is then easily ligated using a ligature device.

The surgeon uses a ligature to dissect fatty tissue below the innominate vein, following the vein to the lima vein. The dissection continues to remove remaining tissue along the pericardial reflection.
The surgeon prepares to insert a sub-xiphoid port, which will provide access for further surgical maneuvers in the thymectomy procedure.
The view from the sub-xiphoid thirty-degree camera shows the surgical field, providing a clear perspective for the ongoing thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the thymus gland completely dissected away from the back of the sternum, with a clear view of the innominate vein. The thymus is visibly removed from the neck region.
The surgical field shows a clear view with minimal remaining fatty tissue, following dissection from the innominate vein.
The surgeon uses the subxiphoid approach to pull the thymus back into the right chest, utilizing the sub-xiphoid port for optimal access and visualization.
The surgeon uses an instrument to grasp the thymus, pulling it back into the right chest, providing a clear view of the left phrenic nerve.
The surgical field displays a clear view of the phrenic nerve, flanked by two small vessels on either side.
The surgeon has removed the left inferior horn from the left chest, dissecting it away from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon applies a ligature around the waist of the thymus, adjacent to the phrenic nerve, securing the tissue in preparation for removal.
The surgeon uses the FlexTech instrument to grasp and lift tissue, bending it out of the way to enhance the view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to secure tissue adjacent to the phrenic nerve, ensuring precise placement and tension.
The surgeon uses a ligashor device to hook and manipulate the phrenic nerve, preparing to secure it with ligation.
The surgeon positions the instrument within a millimeter of the phrenic nerve, demonstrating precise control in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the tip of the instrument to touch the small phrenic vein, directing it towards the nearby lemur vein.
The phrenic nerve is visible, positioned laterally near the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the lemur vein and artery, clearly visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the innominate veins, clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on the lateral part of the surgical field, with visible anatomical structures such as the lemur vein, artery, and phrenic nerve nearby.
The surgeon gently pulls the thymus tissue towards themselves, aiming to make final incisions close to the visible phrenic nerve.
The surgeon grasps and pulls the thymus tissue, revealing the innominate vein, the lima vein, and the phrenic nerve in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs the final division of the thymus tissue, completing the operation.
The surgeon has removed nearly all of the visible fat from the chest cavity, leaving the area clear of adipose tissue.
The surgeon removes tissue through the sub-xiphoid approach, completing the extraction process.
The surgical field shows an eight millimeter hole from the flex decks, a camera port, and a five millimeter hole from the hook ligature positioned within the intercostal spaces.
The camera view is oriented from the right chest, with the patient's head positioned to the left side of the screen.
The surgeon performs a vertical sub-xiphoid incision, facilitating the removal of the thymus gland from the chest cavity.
The surgeon uses a ten millimeter camera to capture the surgical field, providing a clear view of the thymectomy procedure through the intercostal spaces.
A 20 French drain is temporarily placed in the surgical field to assist in re-expanding the lungs.
The surgeon observes the lungs inflating and joining at the midline, indicating successful re-expansion following the procedure.

After applying a positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) of ten for five to ten minutes, the surgeon places the drain underwater to visually check for any bubbling, indicating the absence of air leaks.
The surgeon slowly retracts the drain from the surgical site, observing no bubbling, indicating no air leak, before removing the drain completely and closing the incision.
The final result shows the surgical field after completing the thymectomy, with no visible air leaks and the lungs fully inflated and joined at the midline.
